one_line_takeaway: AI2 is a ROP-style 'action hijacking' attack that makes a RAG agent retrieve and reassemble harmful instructions from its own database via semantically-benign prompts, achieving 84.3% avg hijack ASR while bypassing ~100% of keyword/operation filters that stop conventional prompt-injection and jailbreak attacks.
key_finding: AI2 hijacks agent action plans with an average ASR of 84.30% (best 99.70%) across six LLMs and three domains, while its prompts remain semantically benign enough to almost completely defeat conventional filters (100% bypass of banned-word filters, 98.70% of forbidden-operation filters) and to substantially outperform all 11 injection/jailbreak baselines and the SOTA P2SQL against dedicated LLM-based defenses (59.15%) and paraphrasing (59.75%, vs ~26% for baselines). The domain pattern is informative: medical assistants are the easiest to hijack (avg action-hijack ASR 96.34%) because the LLM leans heavily on retrieved private knowledge, while Text2SQL is hardest (73.77%) because LLMs understand SQL well enough to flag destructive ops. Knowledge extraction is cheap (avg 1.26 queries per knowledge item). PGD is the strongest hijacking-prompt optimizer (avg 77.47%); C&W is weakest (56.10%) due to local optima in the sparse discrete text space. Attack transfers across retrievers even without weights (up to 43.68% when shadow and target share training data). On real LangChain/LlamaIndex/Vanna deployments, final avg ASR is 91.44%.
core_contribution: The paper introduces AI2 (AI Application Injection), a novel 'action hijacking' attack against RAG-equipped LLM-based applications (agents). The key innovation, explicitly modeled on Return-Oriented Programming (ROP), is that instead of embedding overtly harmful instructions into the user prompt (which safety filters catch), AI2 makes the application autonomously retrieve harmful instructions already sitting in its own knowledge database and assemble them into the LLM's context. The attacker's submitted prompt is thus semantically harmless (no banned words, no forbidden operations), yet still coerces the LLM into producing harmful action plans. This is distinct from (and the authors deliberately exclude) memory-poisoning attacks, which require privileged write access to the database; AI2 only reads/extracts from memory. The attack has three phases: (1) a knowledge-extracting attack against the RAG retriever to pull out action-aware knowledge 'gadgets', (2) hijacking-prompt generation that crafts adversarial-token prompts steering the retriever to the target knowledge while carrying an innocuous 'assembly instruction' (e.g., 'do the same operation as the first Knowledge on ...'), and (3) application hijacking that fires these prompts to trigger harmful action plans.
threat_model: Adversary is a malicious external user M with only black-box API access to a victim LLM-based application A (submit text prompts, read responses). No privileged access to the knowledge database (explicitly distinguishing this from memory poisoning) and no access to retriever/LLM learned parameters. Two knowledge tiers are defined: a 'weak attacker' knows nothing about the system, while a 'strong attacker' has partial/full knowledge of the application workflow (e.g., retriever mechanism disclosed in docs, though not weights). The application may deploy safety filters Fs (rule-based banned-word filters, model-based forbidden-operation classifiers) and dedicated defenses (LLM-based detectors, paraphrasers). Attacker goal: craft prompt pT that both (i) triggers the desired harmful action AT and (ii) evades Fs. Concrete targeted harms per domain: code generators (induce misjudged/nonexistent bugs, insert malicious code, block fixes); medical assistants (misdiagnose, prescribe controlled/wrong medications); Text2SQL agents (delete/modify unauthorized data, inject spam, read other users' data). Scope note: the paper studies generation of harmful action plans, not their downstream physical execution by external tools.
limitations: Authors' stated limitations: the attack fundamentally depends on the target harmful action's knowledge being retrievable from the application's database; when action-aware knowledge is absent or hard to retrieve, longer prompts and a higher adversarial-token ratio are needed, reducing stealth and raising detection risk (they propose splitting the attack across multiple steps using short-term memory as future mitigation). As with prior work, the generated harmful actions may not actually be executed by downstream external tools, so real-world impact depends on tool implementation and selection. Reviewer-observed caveats: some 'real-world' targets are self-built applications (for privacy/ethics), not live third-party services; effectiveness drops when the shadow retriever differs from the target (transfer ASR falls to ~24-44%); code-obfuscation attacks against M3E are hampered by tokenizer artifacts (avg 58.78%); and paper has notable typos/inconsistencies (e.g., abstract cites 92.7% avg filter bypass and 99.35% built-in bypass while tables report 100%/98.70%; GPT-3/GPT-4 parameter figures of 175B/170T in Table 2 are dubious).

datasets_used: BugNET (open-source .NET issue tracker, code bug/generation dataset)
MultiSQL (schema-integrated context-dependent Text2SQL dataset with diverse SQL operations)
Huatuo (large-scale medical Q&A; authors used a refined 5,000-sample subset with 5 balanced disease types)
quantitative_results: Action hijacking by domain: Medical 96.34% (highest), Code generator 82.80%, Text2SQL 73.77% (lowest)
Knowledge extraction: avg ASR 68.77%, up to 94.43% (code); by domain Code 94.43%, Text2SQL 70.79%, Medical 41.08%; avg 1.26 queries per knowledge item
Hijacking-prompt generation avg ASR by optimizer: PGD 77.47%, I-FGSM 71.38%, FGSM 70.71%, C&W 56.10%; up to 100% (code on MiniLM)
Dedicated-defense bypass: 59.15% (LLM-based detector), 59.75% (paraphrasing) vs ~26% for baselines
Action2NL retrieval accuracy: Top-1 65.53% avg / Top-4 73.25% avg, vs random Top-1 14.66% / Top-4 18.40%
Retriever transferability: 100% ASR when shadow retriever = target; 43.68% (same training data), 37.41% (same architecture), 27.94%/24.69% (unknown model)
Real-world deployments (LangChain/LlamaIndex/Vanna): overall avg ASR 91.44% (knowledge-fetch 94.41%, hijack 96.85%); e.g., Health-Care 99.32%, Vanna 94.62%, LlamaIndex Text2SQL 95.38%
baselines_compared: Prompt injection: Naive attack, Ignore attack, Escape Characters, Fake Completion, Combined Attack
Jailbreak: Role Play, Disguised Intent, Language Translation, Text Continuation, Code Injection, Cipher
Adversarial optimizers compared internally: FGSM, I-FGSM, PGD, C&W
